summary: World War II deeply affected my family and community. I was born in 1924 and was a junior in high school when the war started, right after Pearl Harbor. The war brought fear and uncertainty, especially for Japanese Americans like us, who faced forced relocation and internment. My parents were stoic and strong throughout, never showing panic despite the hardships we endured during this time.

After my parents divorced, I ended up living in Watsonville, California with my two youngest brothers. I went to high school there and made friends, including a close one named Walter Daley. We tried to assimilate into the community, excelling academically and in sports. Watsonville was a place where I felt somewhat at home before the war disrupted everything.

I was in my junior year of high school when Pearl Harbor was attacked on December 7, 1941. It was a shocking event that changed everything for Japanese Americans like me. Our history teacher tried to calm the class by saying the war wouldn't last long and reminded us not to see Japanese classmates as enemies. Still, the attack marked the beginning of a very difficult period for us.

General John L. DeWitt, whom I sometimes called 'dimwit,' was in charge of supplies and came up with the idea to remove all Japanese Americans from the West Coast. He believed we might aid an invasion, which made no sense to me. His actions led to the forced relocation of thousands of people, including my family, under Executive Order 9066.

Executive Order 9066 was the government order that forced over 120,000 Japanese Americans, including my family, to leave our homes with only two or three weeks' notice. We had to sell our belongings quickly, often at a fraction of their value. This order uprooted our lives and sent us to camps far from California, changing everything for us.

My stepfather had friends in Davis, California, and we thought moving there might help us avoid relocation. Unfortunately, that didn't work out because the government later ordered us to move even further inland. Davis was a hopeful idea that ultimately didn't prevent us from being sent to the camps.

Walter Daley was a good friend I really palled around with while living in Watsonville. We tried to assimilate into the community and be part of the town. He was part of the support system that made life a little easier before the war uprooted us.

I was one of the first to be sent to Tule Lake War Relocation Center, where I was assigned to Block 415 C. Tule Lake later became the segregated camp for those who answered 'no' on the loyalty questionnaire. It was a large camp with about 20,000 people, and life there was organized into blocks and wards, trying to mimic a small town.

My wife ended up at the Rohwer War Relocation Center in Arkansas while I was at Tule Lake and later Topaz. Rohwer was one of the ten camps set up across the country to house Japanese Americans during the war. It was far from California and part of the forced dispersal of families.

Manzanar was one of the two camps in California where Japanese Americans were sent during the war. It was part of the network of camps that included Tule Lake and Topaz. Though I was not at Manzanar, it was a significant place for many in our community during that time.

After answering 'yes' on the loyalty questionnaire, my family and I were moved to Topaz War Relocation Center in Utah. Topaz was where we stayed after being segregated from those who answered 'no.' Life there was difficult, but I even managed to go out on seasonal work leave, which felt like a break from camp life.

The War Relocation Authority was the government agency responsible for managing the camps where we were interned. They organized the camps like small towns with block managers and wards. They also issued the loyalty questionnaire that caused so much division among us.

Cleveland, Ohio came up in the context of the loyalty questionnaire and conscientious objectors, but I did not live there. It was mentioned as part of the broader national situation during the war. My experience was mainly on the West Coast and in the camps across the country.

The Quakers were mentioned as part of the broader community during the war, known for their conscientious objection and support for peace. While I didn't have direct interaction with them, they were part of the environment around the camps and the resistance to war policies. They represented a moral voice during those difficult times.

Raymond Cheek was my music teacher and a conscientious objector who came to teach at the camp rather than go to prison. He quietly encouraged me to reconsider my stance when I was in line to answer the loyalty questionnaire. His words made me think deeply, and ultimately I decided to answer 'yes' to both loyalty questions, which changed the course of my life.
